2605.01829.title: GeoSAE: Geometric Prior-Guided Layer-Wise Sparse Autoencoder Annotation of Brain MRI Foundation Models
2605.01829.paper_summary: Brain MRI foundation models learn rich representations of anatomy, but interpreting what clinical information they encode remains an open problem. Standard sparse autoencoders (SAEs) suffer from severe feature collapse in deep transformer layers, and in Alzheimer's disease (AD) research, aging confounds nearly every clinical variable, making naive annotation unreliable. We propose GeoSAE, a geometry-guided SAE framework that uses the foundation model's learned manifold structure to prevent feature collapse and annotates each surviving feature via age-deconfounded partial correlations. Applied to ~14k T1-weighted MRI scans from the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) and the Australian Imaging biomarkers and Lifestyle (AIBL) datasets, GeoSAE identifies a compact, fully interpretable feature set that predicts mild cognitive impairment (MCI)-to-AD conversion (AUC 0.746) using only 2% of the embedding dimensions, while comorbidity-annotated features achieve only chance-level performance. The identified features replicate across cohorts without retraining (r=0.97) and localize to neuroanatomically distinct regions consistent with Braak staging. This shows that geometry-guided SAEs can extract interpretable, biomarkers from frozen brain MRI foundation models.
2605.01829.paper_summary_zh: 腦部 MRI 基礎模型學習豐富的解剖結構表示，但解釋它們編碼的臨床信息仍然是一個未解決的問題。標準的稀疏自編碼器（SAEs）在深度Transformer層中遭受嚴重的特徵崩潰，而在阿茲海默症（AD）研究中，老化幾乎會干擾每一個臨床變數，使得天真的標註變得不可靠。我們提出了 GeoSAE，一種幾何引導的 SAE 框架，利用基礎模型學習的流形結構來防止特徵崩潰，並通過年齡去混淆的部分相關性對每個存活的特徵進行標註。應用於來自阿茲海默症神經影像倡議（ADNI）和澳大利亞影像生物標記與生活方式（AIBL）數據集的約 14,000 例 T1 加權 MRI 掃描，GeoSAE 識別出一組緊湊且完全可解釋的特徵集，僅使用 2% 的嵌入維度即可預測輕度認知障礙（MCI）轉變為阿茲海默症（AUC 0.746），而合併症標註的特徵僅達到偶然水平的表現。所識別的特徵在不同隊列中無需重新訓練即可複製（r=0.97），並定位於與 Braak 分期一致的神經解剖學上不同的區域。這顯示幾何引導的 SAEs 可以從凍結的腦部 MRI 基礎模型中提取可解釋的生物標記。
2605.01829.author: Favour Nerrise et.al.
2605.01829.authors: Favour Nerrise, Lucy Yin, Mohammad H. Abbasi, Kilian M. Pohl, Ehsan Adeli
